tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 insomnia task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of insomnia as the working diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and frames it as a clinical diagnosis (e.g., acute vs chronic if duration is known).
Explains insomnia in plain language, including that it involves difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep, or early-morning awakening with daytime impairment.
Explains first-line treatment and must explicitly include both "CBT-I" and "melatonin" as treatment options (with CBT-I presented as first-line).
Assesses basic insomnia history: onset/duration, sleep schedule, sleep latency, awakenings, early awakening, total sleep time, daytime consequences, and precipitating stressors.

Screens for common contributors/secondary causes: caffeine/nicotine/alcohol, shift work/jet lag, poor sleep hygiene, medication/substance contributors, pain, nocturia, GERD, and environmental factors.

Screens for red flags/alternate diagnoses that change management: suicidality or severe depression, mania/hypomania symptoms, obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, excessive daytime sleepiness), restless legs symptoms, and parasomnias.
Does not prescribe any medication in this L0 diagnosis-only task; if the agent discusses possible meds, it remains educational and non-prescriptive.

If the agent does propose prescribing (despite task level), it performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about allergies (critical) and contraindications (critical), and also addresses drug interactions (non-critical).

Provides clear self-care guidance consistent with CBT-I principles (stimulus control, sleep restriction concepts, consistent wake time, limiting naps, reducing evening screens/caffeine) without unsafe claims.

Sets expectations and timeline: notes CBT-I takes weeks, melatonin is most helpful for circadian-related issues and should be timed appropriately, and advises follow-up if symptoms persist or red flags are present.

Clarify duration and context (acute vs chronic; stressors; schedule changes).
Review sleep habits and substances/medications that can disrupt sleep.

Explain the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia".

steps: Gather insomnia symptom pattern and quantify sleep (latency, awakenings, total sleep time).

Screen for OSA/RLS/circadian rhythm disorder/psychiatric contributors.

Provide safety-net advice: urgent care for suicidality/mania; evaluation for OSA if suspected.

tool_call_sequence.description: Somatostatin analog therapy (e.g., octreotide) is first-line for controlling hormone-mediated symptoms and can stabilize disease; prescribing must include required safety checks before ordering.

nl_assertions: Clearly explains the diagnosis using the terms "glucagonoma" and "glucagon", linking excess glucagon to key clinical features (e.g., hyperglycemia/diabetes, weight loss, necrolytic migratory erythema).
Explains treatment using the terms "octreotide" and "surgery", distinguishing symptomatic control (somatostatin analog) from definitive therapy (resection when feasible).
Performs an allergy check before prescribing and documents the result; if allergy is present, avoids octreotide and proposes an appropriate alternative plan and specialist input.

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing (e.g., clinically relevant bradyarrhythmia risk, uncontrolled gallbladder disease considerations, pregnancy/breastfeeding context as applicable) and adapts the plan if contraindications exist.

Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing (non-critical) and addresses any major interactions or monitoring needs (e.g., effects on glucose control, additive bradycardia with rate-limiting agents).

Addresses immediate complications and supportive care needs in counseling (glycemic management, nutrition, skin care for rash, thromboembolism risk assessment when clinically indicated) without making unsafe or unsupported claims.

Recommends appropriate staging/confirmation strategy in narrative (e.g., fasting glucagon level, pancreatic imaging, somatostatin receptor imaging, biopsy when indicated) and frames these as specialist-coordinated when not directly ordering tests.
Uses shared decision-making and sets expectations about prognosis and next steps, including urgency for specialist referral given malignancy risk and potential metastases.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose glucagonoma, initiate somatostatin analog therapy with required safety checks, and refer for definitive surgical and multidisciplinary evaluation.
must_collect: history of characteristic rash (necrolytic migratory erythema) or photos/description
steps: Synthesize presentation and available data consistent with glucagonoma (functional pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor secreting glucagon).
Explain diagnosis and mechanism (excess glucagon) and relate to symptoms.
Before prescribing: complete allergy_check and contraindication_check (critical) and drug_interaction_check (non-critical); document outcomes.
Prescribe octreotide for symptom control and biochemical improvement; include monitoring considerations (glucose, gallbladder, GI effects).
Refer to endocrinology and surgical oncology/HPB surgery for staging, resectability assessment, and definitive management including surgery.

acceptable_variants.description: Same core outcomes, but referral is explicitly to a multidisciplinary neuroendocrine tumor (NET) center and includes oncology for metastatic/systemic therapy planning.
steps: Record diagnosis: glucagonoma.
Perform required pre-prescribing safety checks; document.
Prescribe octreotide (short-acting bridge to long-acting formulation per protocol).
Refer to NET multidisciplinary team (endocrinology, HPB surgery, medical oncology) for staging and definitive therapy including surgery.

acceptable_variants.description: If octreotide is not suitable (e.g., allergy/contraindication), the agent avoids prescribing it and prioritizes urgent specialist referral while documenting safety rationale; still meets L3 outcome structure by prescribing an appropriate alternative supportive medication when safe (e.g., glucose-lowering therapy already indicated) and not claiming it treats the tumor.
steps: Record diagnosis: glucagonoma.
Identify and document allergy/contraindication to octreotide during pre-prescribing checks.

Prescribe an appropriate supportive medication based on patient need and safety (e.g., diabetes therapy adjustment) with required safety checks, clarifying it is supportive and not definitive tumor therapy.
Refer urgently to endocrinology and HPB surgery/NET center for definitive management and alternative tumor-directed options.
